system_prompt:
You are distilling one chapter of a guitar method book into a short summary.

The user will give you a chapter file with topic-organized verbatim quotes
plus the human-rationale notes for each. Write a one-paragraph summary (4-7
sentences) that captures:

  - what this chapter teaches (the techniques, concepts, rules, examples)
  - the master's characteristic moves visible in this chapter (if any)
  - the load-bearing terminology this chapter introduces

Reference the topic labels from the chapter file when useful. Do not invent
information not present in the chapter. If the chapter is biographical or
introductory rather than instructional, say so explicitly.

Output just the paragraph — no preamble, no JSON, no markdown formatting.
user_prompt:
Chapter 14: Chord Melodies

<<<CHAPTER FILE
# Chapter 14 — Chord Melodies

_Pages 75-79._

> **Verbatim-quote constraint.** The quotes below are exact substrings of the raw transcript produced by `pdftotext`. The transcript may contain extraction artifacts where the book's typeface confuses the tool — e.g. `7irst` or `<irst` for `first`, `speci7ic` for `specific`. **Do NOT manually fix these in this file**: the quote-fidelity validator compares against the raw transcript, so a 'fixed' quote will fail verification and break Stage 2 idempotency. If a future pipeline iteration adds artifact-normalization, it must normalize the transcript and these files together.

## m(maj7) 7-tone voice leading rule

> when using m? type chords with m7 type chords of the same root, the 7 TONE SHOULD
> MOVE UP 1 FRET or DOWN 1 FRET INTO A ROOT OR 67 TONE.

**Significance (p. 74).** Voice-leading rule for the m(maj7) → m7 transition.

## Chord melody definition

> the term chord melody refers to the playing of the melody AND the chords of a song at
> the same time.

**Significance (p. 75).** Defines the chapter's central technique.

## Raise melody an octave rule

> It is almost always necessary to raise the melody one octave higher for chord melodies.

**Significance (p. 75).** Mechanical precondition: melody must be high enough to sit on top of chord.

## Melody-to-chord-tone correspondence

> The melody usually corresponds to one of the tones of the basic chord that is written above it, or
> to one of the tones of the extensions or altered chords with the same root as the basic chord.

**Significance (p. 75).** Foundational principle linking melody notes to playable chord forms.

## Melody on top

> the melody is always played ABOVE the chord, not in the middle or in
> the bass.

**Significance (p. 75).** Voicing rule positioning melody as soprano.

## Chord every few notes

> When harmonizing a melody (that is, playing a chord melody) in a simple manner, chords are
> generally played every few notes, not on every note.

**Significance (p. 76).** Pedagogical entry-point: sparse chord placement.

## 1st note of measure as chord anchor

> the 1ST NOTE IN EACH
> MEASURE

**Significance (p. 76).** Identifies the conventional chord-anchor position.

## Relative majors/minors substitution

> USING RELATIVE MAJORS AND MINORS

**Significance (p. 76).** Names the chapter's first chord-melody enhancement technique.

## Relative chords by key signature

> any chords that have the same
> key signature are called relative chords.

**Significance (p. 76).** Defines relative chord pairs via shared key signature.

## Top-note relationship analysis

> ANALYZE THE
> RELATIONSHIP OF THE MELODY NOTE TO THE CHORD AND FIND A CHORD THAT HAS
> THIS RELATIONSHIP ON TOP.

**Significance (p. 76).** Core search procedure for chord-melody voicings.

## Picardy-third ending

> Ending on a major is an often used technique in minor songs.

**Significance (p. 77).** Names the Picardy-third move as a standard ending.

## Back-cycling defined

> This technique of putting in V chords right before another chord will be
> called back-cycling

**Significance (p. 78).** Defines back-cycling — preparing each chord with its V.

CHAPTER FILE>>>
